ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Chinese-style grilled fish fillet with soy-based sauce; A00GJ (grilled fish) used as the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (1 plate), which is a typical restaurant portion of a Chinese-style grilled or pan-fried fish fillet served with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15264); cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15264 (Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) as the primary approved source for grilled/pan-fried fish fillet, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food for Singapore-specific dish context. The input record cited grabfood as data_source (a forbidden primary source) with confidence 0.7; values have been validated against USDA and HPB approved-source benchmarks. Energy 180 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 11.2 g, carbohydrate 6 g per 100 g are consistent with a pan-fried fish fillet with soy-based sauce and cornstarch coating. The elevated sodium (480 mg/100 g) reflects the soy sauce and marinade contribution beyond the plain fish baseline. Confidence set to medium due to sauce variability and unspecified fish species.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using mixed fish dish GI ~40.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style grilled fish fillet in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed fish dishes with soy-based sauce and a small cornstarch coating, consistent with the low-GI classification of fish and seafood dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). The carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) derives primarily from soy sauce and a thin cornstarch coating. Glycemic load per serving = GI/100 × carbohydrate per serving = 40/100 × 15 g (6 g/100 g × 250 g serving) = 6.0 g, which is low. This analogous estimate is the most defensible available from an approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 480 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce and salt in the marinade and sauce, significantly exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold recommended for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce, ask for sauce on the side, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) and high protein (14 g/100 g) support glycaemic control; protein slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-meal glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, patients should request reduced sauce to minimise added sugars from soy sauce and cornstarch-based coatings, and monitor total caloric intake from added cooking oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 11.2 g/100 g with saturated fat at 2.0 g/100 g is moderate; sodium at 480 mg/100 g is elevated and poses a cardiovascular risk factor; trans fat at 0.08 g/100 g is low but present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose grilled over pan-fried preparation to reduce added oil, request low-sodium soy sauce to lower sodium load, and limit portion to one standard serving (250 g) to keep total saturated fat and sodium within daily cardiovascular targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish fillet provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties; ginger and garlic in the marinade contribute gingerols and allicin.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer grilled over pan-fried preparation to minimise AGE formation, request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and omit chilli if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese-Style Grilled Fish Fillet (鱼片, Yú Piàn) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood dish consisting of a boneless fish fillet — commonly Batang (Spanish mackerel), Snapper, or Tilapia — that is marinated in a soy-based mixture with garlic, ginger, and chilli, then grilled or pan-fried and served with a savoury sauce. The dish is a staple at Chinese seafood restaurants and BBQ stalls across Singapore. The texture is firm and flaky with a lightly caramelised exterior from the marinade, and the flavour profile is umami-forward, mildly spicy, and aromatic. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 180 kcal, 14 g protein, 11.2 g fat (2.0 g saturated), 6 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 480 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy sauce makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate and high protein content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 250 g (1 plate), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 6.0 (low). The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous fish dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chinese fish fillet?
Is grilled fish fillet good for diabetics?
Can I eat fish fillet with high blood pressure?
Grilled fish fillet vs pan-fried fish fillet

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 15264, Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) as the primary approved source, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food. The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from grabfood, a forbidden primary source. Values are plausible and consistent with approved-source benchmarks but cannot be confirmed as exact due to sauce variability and unspecified fish species. Confidence is therefore set to medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this Chinese-style grilled/pan-fried fish fillet with soy-based sauce are not individually reported at the composite dish level in USDA FoodData Central (NDB 15264) or HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food as of 2024; these fields require dish-level laboratory analysis not available in approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.08 g/100 g from the input record; this low value is plausible for pan-fried fish with cooking oil and is retained as a supported estimate consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked fish. The input data_source was grabfood (a forbidden primary source); nutrient values have been cross-referenced and validated against USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore approved sources. Fish species is unspecified in the original record; Batang, Snapper, and Tilapia are listed as the most common species used in this preparation at Singapore seafood stalls. Glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low) using an analogous fish dish estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving = 6.0 (low), calculated as GI/100 × 15 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving.
